Building Distributed Systems with Aspire

From local dev to cloud in minutes — with Aspire 13.2

Tamir Dresher

Principal Engineer @ Microsoft Security
.NET Bond Leadership

tamirdresher.com | aspire.dev

March 30, 2026

Welcome everyone! My name is Tamir Dresher. I'm a Principal Engineer at Microsoft Security, and I've been shipping distributed systems for over 15 years. Today, I want to show you something that will change how you build those systems: Aspire.

The Problem: Local Dev Complexity

Traditional Approach

  • 200-line Docker Compose files
  • Hardcoded connection strings
  • Manual port management (5432, 6379...)
  • Service discovery headaches
  • Environment variable chaos
  • Rewrite everything for production

The Mess

😫
  • API: localhost:5000
  • PostgreSQL: localhost:5432
  • Redis: localhost:6379
  • RabbitMQ: localhost:5672
  • Elasticsearch: localhost:9200
You're building a real-world app. You need an API, database, cache, message queue. Traditionally, you write a 200-line Docker Compose file, hardcode connection strings, and hope nothing conflicts. That Docker Compose file is useless for production — you'll rewrite it in Kubernetes. Your system's intent is buried in YAML.

eShop as an Example

https://github.com/dotnet/eshop

The eShop reference application is the canonical example of Aspire in action. This is the real architecture — multiple services, databases, caches, all orchestrated by Aspire.

eShop as an Example

https://github.com/dotnet/eshop

Here you can see the eShop running in the Aspire dashboard. Every service, every dependency, all visible and monitored.

eShop as an Example

Making it production grade: reliability, health checks, logging, telemetry etc

https://github.com/dotnet/eshop

The eShop shows production-grade patterns: health checks, structured logging, OpenTelemetry tracing, resilience policies. All powered by Aspire ServiceDefaults.

Code Example: eShop Simplified

var builder = DistributedApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

// Infrastructure services
var postgres = builder.AddPostgres("postgres");
var redis = builder.AddRedis("cache");

// Application services
var catalogDb = builder.AddPostgresDatabase("catalog-db", postgres);
var catalogApi = builder.AddProject<Projects.CatalogService>("catalog")
    .WithReference(catalogDb)
    .WithReference(redis);

var orderingApi = builder.AddProject<Projects.OrderingService>("ordering")
    .WithReference(postgres)
    .WithReference(redis);

var gateway = builder.AddProject<Projects.ApiGateway>("gateway")
    .WithReference(catalogApi)
    .WithReference(orderingApi);

builder.Build().Run();

20 lines = 200+ lines of Docker Compose

This is a simplified eShop AppHost. Infrastructure first: Postgres and Redis. Then databases as first-class resources. Dependencies are explicit with WithReference. No hardcoded strings — Aspire injects connection strings automatically. When you run this, Postgres and Redis start, databases are created, environment variables are injected, and services discover each other by name. The Dashboard shows the entire dependency graph.

What Is Aspire?

Aspire AppHost
(Your C# or TypeScript code)
↓
Developer Control Plane
(Orchestration Layer)
↓
Local: Docker
Cloud: Azure/K8s
Define once. Run locally. Deploy to cloud.
Aspire is Microsoft's orchestration framework for distributed applications. You define your entire system in a single AppHost file. Aspire's Developer Control Plane handles port allocation, environment variables, health checks, and service discovery. The same AppHost works locally and generates deployment manifests for Azure or Kubernetes.

Aspire Architecture: The Building Blocks

Layer 1: AppHost

var builder = DistributedApplication
    .CreateBuilder(args);

var db = builder.AddPostgres("postgres");
var cache = builder.AddRedis("cache");

var api = builder
    .AddProject<Projects.MyApi>("api")
    .WithReference(db)
    .WithReference(cache);

builder.Build().Run();

Layer 2: DCP

  • ✅ Allocates ports dynamically
  • ✅ Injects environment variables
  • ✅ Starts containers in order
  • ✅ Monitors health checks
  • ✅ Exposes REST API

Layer 3: Runtime

  • Docker/Podman containers
  • Local processes (Node, Python)
Aspire has three layers. Layer 1 is your AppHost where you declare resources declaratively. Layer 2 is the Developer Control Plane that reads your declarations, allocates ports, injects variables, and manages startup order. Layer 3 delegates to Docker for containers and manages local processes. The Dashboard visualizes everything with live logs, health status, and OpenTelemetry traces.

New in Aspire 13.2: TypeScript AppHost

C# AppHost
.NET developers
+
TypeScript AppHost
JavaScript developers
Same model. JavaScript syntax. Node.js runtime. No .NET SDK required.
  • ✅ Polyglot teams can collaborate
  • ✅ No .NET SDK needed for TypeScript AppHost
  • ✅ Orchestrate Python + Node.js + .NET together
  • ✅ Lower contributor friction for open source
Aspire 13.2 lets you write the AppHost in TypeScript. Same orchestration model, same dashboard, same deployment — but now JavaScript developers can author infrastructure without touching C#. This matters for polyglot teams. Python, JavaScript, and .NET developers can all modify the AppHost without friction. If your services are Node.js and your AppHost is TypeScript, you don't need the .NET SDK at all.

TypeScript AppHost: Polyglot Example

import { createBuilder } from '@microsoft/aspire';

const builder = await createBuilder();

// Python RAG service
const ragService = await builder.addPythonApp('rag-service', './services/rag')
    .withEnvironment('MODEL_PATH', '/models/llama-2')
    .withVolume('./models', '/models');

// Node.js coordinator
const coordinator = await builder.addNodeApp('coordinator', './services/coordinator')
    .withReference(ragService);

// .NET API Gateway
const gateway = await builder.addProject('gateway', './services/gateway')
    .withReference(coordinator);

// Redis for distributed caching
const redis = await builder.addRedis('cache');
coordinator.withReference(redis);
gateway.withReference(redis);

await builder.build().run();

Python + Node.js + .NET orchestrated seamlessly

This example shows TypeScript AppHost orchestrating Python, Node.js, and .NET services. Service discovery works across language boundaries. The Dashboard shows health and logs for all services. Deployment to Kubernetes generates manifests for all three containers automatically. This is incredibly powerful for enterprise transformation and AI integration.

Hands-On Demonstration

Demo Environment

  • VS Code with AppHost
  • Run aspire run
  • Aspire Dashboard at localhost:18888
  • Watch services start
  • View logs and traces
  • Test API through dashboard

Key Points to Highlight

  • No Docker Compose needed — just aspire run
  • Automatic port allocation
  • Connection strings injected transparently
  • Dashboard shows all services in one place
  • Health checks + distributed tracing built-in
Live demo: Start a simple project with an API and database. Run aspire run and show the Dashboard. Make an API request through the dashboard. Show logs from the service. Demonstrate service discovery — the API finds the database automatically. This is the dev experience Aspire gives you. Imagine scaling this to 20 services with health checks, logging, and tracing built-in.

Service Discovery & Configuration

Declaration

var postgres = builder
    .AddPostgres("postgres");
var db = builder
    .AddPostgresDatabase("catalog-db", postgres);

var api = builder
    .AddProject<Projects.CatalogApi>("api")
    .WithReference(db);

Automatic Injection

ConnectionStrings__CatalogDb=
Server=localhost;Port=5432;...

In Your Code

configuration
    .GetConnectionString("CatalogDb")
No hardcoding. No manual port tracking. No broken service discovery at 2 AM.
One of Aspire's superpowers is automatic service discovery. When you declare a resource and another service depends on it, Aspire allocates a port, generates connection strings, and injects environment variables. Cross-language service discovery works the same way. In TypeScript AppHost, RAG_URL is automatically set for dependent services. No hardcoding, no manual port tracking.

Deployment: From Local to Cloud

AppHost (dev)
Runs locally with DCP
→
Same AppHost
Generates manifests
→
Cloud
Azure / Kubernetes

Deployment Command

# Local development
dotnet run

# Deploy to Azure Container Apps
aspire deploy --manifest <manifest> --output-path ./deploy

No manual Kubernetes YAML. No separate deployment scripts.

The same AppHost that runs locally generates deployment manifests for the cloud. Aspire can generate Docker Compose, Kubernetes manifests, Azure Container Instances configs, or Azure Container Apps configs. The deploy command builds container images, uploads to a registry, creates Azure resources (databases, caches), and deploys services with the correct environment variables and dependencies. All from your AppHost.

The Aspire Ecosystem

Databases
PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, MongoDB, CosmosDB
Caches
Redis, Valkey
Messaging
RabbitMQ, Service Bus, Kafka
Search
Elasticsearch
Observability
OpenTelemetry, App Insights
Cloud
Azure Container Apps, AKS, CosmosDB
Community
Dapr, Vault, Kind (K8s)

Want Redis? builder.AddRedis(). Aspire handles the rest.

Aspire's strength is the ecosystem. Built-in integrations for databases, caches, messaging, search, observability, and Azure services. Community integrations include Dapr, HashiCorp Vault, and our own Kind integration for Kubernetes clusters. You can build sophisticated systems without writing infrastructure code. This is the future: not Terraform, not Ansible — just clean, declarative code that runs locally exactly as it will in production.

AI-Powered Development with Aspire

🤖 Aspire CLI & MCP

  • aspire run / build / add — CLI-first workflow
  • MCP Server — AI agents query resources, logs, traces
  • aspire doctor — environment diagnostics
  • aspire add redis — one command integrations

🔌 Copilot Integration

  • Aspire MCP tools in GitHub Copilot
  • Query dashboard from chat
  • AI-assisted troubleshooting

🧪 Testing & Isolation

  • Detached mode — aspire run --detach
  • Isolated tests — DistributedApplicationTestingBuilder
  • Spin up full app in integration tests
  • Real containers, real connections

⚡ Developer Inner Loop

  • Hot reload — code changes without restart
  • Dashboard API — programmatic access
  • Custom commands — WithCommand()
  • Interaction service — UI prompts from code
Aspire 13.2 is deeply AI-friendly. The CLI supports MCP (Model Context Protocol), letting AI agents like GitHub Copilot query your running app — resources, logs, traces, metrics — all from chat. Detached mode lets you run Aspire in the background while AI tools interact with it. The testing builder creates isolated app instances for integration tests with real containers. Custom commands and the interaction service let you add one-click actions to the dashboard.

Key Takeaways

1. Aspire unifies your infrastructure: One AppHost defines everything — local dev, cloud deployment, observability.
2. Local dev becomes effortless: No Docker Compose wrestling. dotnet run handles it.
3. Service discovery is automatic: Your services find each other by name. Connection strings are injected transparently.
4. TypeScript AppHost opens polyglot orchestration: JavaScript developers can author infrastructure. Python + Node.js + .NET orchestrate seamlessly.
5. Deployment is intent-based: Same AppHost generates manifests for local Docker, Kubernetes, or Azure.
6. Observability is built-in: OpenTelemetry, structured logging, and health checks are core, not afterthoughts.
For years, we've used Docker Compose for local dev and Kubernetes YAML for production. They're fundamentally different, so we maintained both. Aspire collapses that separation. You write your system once, and Aspire adapts it to your environment. That's the future of distributed systems development.

Getting Started & Resources

First Steps

  1. Install .NET 10 SDK + Docker
  2. dotnet new aspire-starter
  3. aspire run
  4. Explore the Dashboard at localhost:18888

Documentation

  • aspire.dev — Official docs
  • github.com/microsoft/aspire — Source

Session Resources

  • 📂 Session demos repo
  • 🛠️ Aspire workshop (hands-on)
  • 📝 tamirdresher.com — Blog

Key CLI Commands

aspire run              # start app
aspire run --detach     # background mode
aspire add redis        # add integration
aspire doctor           # check environment
Here are all the resources to get started. The session demos are at the GitHub repo — all 8 examples we walked through today. The workshop repo has guided exercises you can do on your own. My blog has deep-dive articles on Aspire patterns. Aspire has moved to github.com/microsoft/aspire — that's where the source and issues live now.
